The proof for each of the claim will be provided in the exercises:

  • An integer is divisible by \(2^j\) if the last \(j\) digits are divisible by \(2^j\).
  • An integer is divisible by \(5^j\) if the last \(j\) digits are divisible by \(5^j\).
  • An integer is divisible by 3 or 9 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 3 or 9, respectively.
  • An integer is divisible by 7, 11 or 13 if the alternating sum of blocks of three digits is divisible by 7, 11 or 13 respectively. For example, 4896927 is divisible by 7 because (927 - 896 + 004 =) 35 is divisible by 7.
  • An integer is divisible by 11 if the integer obtained by alternately adding and substracting the digits is divisible by 11. For example, 918082 is divisible by 11 because [9 - 1 + 8 - 0 + 8 - 2] = 22, which is divisible by 11.
Prove that if \(d|b\), then \((a_k \ldots a_1 a_0)_b\) is divisible by \(d^j\) if and only if \((a_{j-1} \ldots a_1 a_0)_b\) is divisible by \(d^j\).
FETCH:number_theory/divisibility_rules/divisibility_rules_for_2_and_5
Determine the hight power of 2 that divides 1,423,408.

2 divides 8, so it divides 1,423,408.

4 divides 08, so it divides 1,423,408.

8 divides 408, so it divides 1,423,408.

16 divides 3408, so it divides 1,423,408.

32 does not divides 23408, so it does not divides 1,423,408.

Therefore, the highest power is 4.
Determine the hight power of 5 that divides 112,250.
Since \(5^3 | 250\) but \(5^4 | 2250\). The highest power that can divide 112,250 is \(5^3\).
What is the highest power of 2 that divides \((101111110)_2\)?

Since \(2|2\), then \((101111110)_2\) is divisible by \(2^j\) if the last \(j\) digits are divisible by \(2^j\).

\(2^1 | (0)_2\) but \(2^2 \nmid (10)_2\), so the highest power is 1.
Prove that an integer is divisible by 3 or 9 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 3 or 9 respectively.
FETCH:number_theory/divisibility_rules/divisibility_rules_for_3_and_9
Is 987,654,321 divisible by 3? If so, is it divisible by 9?

Since:

$$\begin{gather}9+8+7+6+5+4+3+2+1 = 45\end{gather}$$

then the integer is divisible by 9, which means it's also divisible by 3.
Prove that if \(d|(b-1)\), then \(d|(a_k \ldots a_1 a_0)_b\) if and only if \(d|(a_k + \cdots + a_1 + a_0)\).
FETCH:number_theory/divisibility_rules/divisibility_rules_for_3_and_9_general

If \(2|(3-1)\), then 2 divides \((1210122)_3\) if it divides the sum of the digits in base 10. Since:

$$\begin{gather} 1+2+1+0+1+2+2 = 9 \end{gather}$$

and \(2 \nmid 9\), then 2 does not divide \((1210122)_3\).

If \(2|(3-1)\), then 2 divides \((1112201112)_3\) if it divides the sum of the digits in base 10. Since:

$$\begin{gather} 1+1+1+2+2+0+1+1+1+2 = 12\end{gather}$$

and \(2 \nmid 12\), then 2 does not divide \((1112201112)_3\).
Prove that an integer is divisible by 11 if the integer obtained by alternately adding and substracting the digits is divisible by 11.
FETCH:number_theory/divisibility_rules/divisibility_rule_for_11

Since:

$$\begin{gather} 6-7+4-3+1-0+9-7+6-3+7-5 = 8 \end{gather}$$

and 8 is not divisible by 11, then 674,310,976,375 is not divisible by 11.
Is 1,086,320,015 divisible by 11?

Since:

$$\begin{gather} 1-0+8-6+3-2+0-0+1-5 = 0 \end{gather}$$

then 1,086,320,015 is divisible by 11.
Prove that an integer is divisible by 7, 11 or 13 if the alternating sum of blocks of three digits is divisible by 7, 11 or 13 respectively.
FETCH:number_theory/divisibility_rules/divisibility_rule_for_7_11_13
Is 59,358,208 divisible by 7, 11 or 13?

Since:

$$\begin{gather} 208 - 358 + 59 = -91 \end{gather}$$

and -91 is divisible by 7 and 13, but not 11, then 59,358,208 is divisible by 7 and 13 only.
Prove that if \(d|(b+1)\), then \(d|(a_k \ldots a_1 a_0)_b\) if and only if \(d|((-1)^k a_k + ... + a_2 - a_1 + a_0)\).
FETCH:number_theory/divisibility_rules/divisibility_rules_base_plus_1
